message: docs: expand engine and wasm demo copy for clarity

files.patch:
@@ -20,34 +20,29 @@ export function OpenSourceEngines() {

- wasm_cmaes is a Rust implementation of CMA-ES compiled to WebAssembly with - wasm-bindgen. It comes with: + Rust CMA-ES compiled to WebAssembly, wrapped in a clean JS/TS API and paired with a D3 + + Tailwind playground. Drop a serious optimizer into any browser tool and watch the + covariance ellipsoid adapt live.

    -
  • A clean JS/TS API (fmin, batch runner, ask/tell loop).
    • -
  • - Two builds: sequential (pkg/) and SIMD/Rayon-enabled (pkg-par/) - for real parallelism where browsers allow it. -
    • -
  • - A D3 + Tailwind playground (examples/viz-benchmarks.html) that lets you watch - CMA-ES chew through classic test functions in real time. -
    • +
  • Two bundles: `pkg/` sequential and `pkg-par/` with SIMD + Rayon for threaded browsers.
    • +
  • Ask/tell loop, batch API, deterministic seeded RNG, JSON state save/restore.
    • +
  • Benchmark dashboard to compare against a naive μ+λ ES and see the “metric learning” effect.
-

- You can load it directly in a browser app: -

+

Minimal JS usage:

 {`import init, { fmin } from "./pkg/cmaes_wasm.js";
 
-await init(); // initializes the WASM module
+await init();
 
-const start = new Float64Array([3.0, -2.0]);
-const sigma0 = 0.8;
-
-const res = fmin(start, sigma0, (x) => x[0] * x[0] + x[1] * x[1]);
+const res = fmin(new Float64Array([3, -2]), 0.8, x => x[0]*x[0] + x[1]*x[1]);
 console.log(res.best_f, res.best_x());`}
+

+ To rebuild or teach with the full viz: scripts/build-all.sh, then open + examples/viz-benchmarks.html; parallelism requires wasm threads + atomics + enabled. +

- fast_cmaes pushes hard on performance while still feeling like a very normal - Python library: + A hyper-optimized Rust core with a friendly Python surface. Feels like + scipy.optimize, runs like a modern ES: SIMD, Rayon, deterministic seeds, + restarts, constraints, vectorized objectives, and a Rich TUI.

    -
  • - Published to PyPI as fast-cmaes (module name fastcma). -
    • -
  • - Multiple entry points: fmin, fmin_vec, fmin_constrained, - and a configurable CMAES class. -
    • -
  • - SIMD-accelerated dot products, optional Rayon-based parallel objective evaluation, - full/diagonal covariance modes, restart helpers, deterministic seeds. -
    • -
  • - A Rich-powered TUI demo so you can literally watch σ and best-f evolve in your - terminal. -
    • +
  • PyPI: fast-cmaes (module fastcma); wheels for Linux/macOS/Windows.
    • +
  • APIs: fmin, fmin_vec, fmin_constrained, and a configurable CMAES class.
    • +
  • Full or diagonal covariance, restart helpers, seeded runs, optional LAPACK eigensolver and NumPy fast paths.
    • +
  • Rich-powered terminal UI to watch σ, best-f, and eval counts live.
-

Installation and minimal usage:

+

Minimal Python usage:

 {`python -m pip install fast-cmaes
 
 from fastcma import fmin
 
 def sphere(x):
-    return sum(v * v for v in x)
+    return sum(v*v for v in x)
 
-xmin, es = fmin(sphere, [0.5, -0.2, 0.8],
-                sigma=0.3,
-                maxfevals=4000,
-                ftarget=1e-12)
+xmin, es = fmin(sphere, [0.5, -0.2, 0.8], sigma=0.3, maxfevals=4000, ftarget=1e-12)
 print("xmin", xmin)`}

- Under the hood the Python API is just driving the same Rust core that powers the benchmarks - and TUI. The whole point is to get “serious” CMA-ES performance without giving up the - convenience of simple Python callables as objectives. + Build/dev loop: maturin develop --release (optionally --features numpy_support,eigen_lapack), or run + ./scripts/setup_and_demo.sh to spin up a uv env and launch the Rich TUI. +

+

+ Practical limit note: full CMA-ES is O(n²) inside; switch to the diagonal mode for cheap, + very high-dimensional problems, or keep full covariance when each evaluation is costly and + geometry learning matters most.

@@ -10,6 +10,11 @@ export function WasmDemo() { CMA-ES playground directly into the browser. The panel below is just your wasm_cmaes viz, wired in as a static asset under public/wasm-demo/.

+

+ Rebuild it yourself with scripts/build-all.sh; parallelism needs wasm threads + and atomics enabled. The dashboard lets you toggle benchmark functions, seeds, and + population sizes to see how the covariance ellipsoid learns the metric in real time. +
